title: Cost Allocation
description: Quiz covering cost allocation, direct and indirect costs, cost objects, cost centres and allocation bases.

questions.question: What is the primary purpose of cost allocation?
options: To assign costs to products, services, departments or projects
questions.explanation: Cost allocation assigns costs to cost objects so managers can understand the true cost of operating the business.

questions.question: Which of the following is a cost object?
questions.explanation: A cost object is anything for which managers want to measure costs, including products, services, customers and projects.

questions.question: Which statement best describes a direct cost?
options: A cost that can be traced directly to a specific cost object
A cost that benefits the entire company
A cost that must always be estimated
questions.explanation: Direct costs can be identified specifically with one product, service or project.

questions.question: Which of the following is typically an indirect cost?
options: Special equipment rented for one project
questions.explanation: Factory rent supports the entire production facility and cannot easily be traced to one product.

questions.question: Indirect costs are often referred to as:
questions.explanation: Indirect costs are commonly called overhead because they support multiple activities rather than one specific cost object.

questions.question: What is an allocation base?
options: A factor used to distribute indirect costs among cost objects
The location where products are manufactured
questions.explanation: Allocation bases determine how indirect costs are shared between different cost objects.

questions.question: Which of the following is commonly used as an allocation base?
questions.explanation: Machine hours, labor hours and production volume are all common allocation bases.

questions.question: Why can't factory rent normally be assigned directly to a single product?
options: Because it supports the production of multiple products
Because it is a variable cost
Because it only occurs once per year
questions.explanation: Factory rent benefits the entire facility and therefore must be allocated.

questions.question: A company spends $500 of wood to manufacture one dining table. The wood should be classified as:
questions.explanation: The wood can be traced directly to one table, making it a direct cost.

questions.question: Which statement best explains why businesses allocate indirect costs?
options: To better estimate the true cost of products and services
To reduce the number of departments
questions.explanation: Allocating indirect costs provides a more complete picture of what products and services actually cost.

questions.question: What is the primary purpose of a cost centre?
options: To collect costs before they are allocated to cost objects
questions.explanation: Cost centres accumulate costs so they can later be allocated to products, services or other cost objects.

questions.question: Which of the following is most likely to be a cost centre?
questions.explanation: Departments such as maintenance, IT and administration commonly function as cost centres.

questions.question: A factory allocates electricity costs based on machine hours. Why is machine hours an appropriate allocation base?
options: Because electricity consumption is closely related to machine usage
Because machine hours always equal labor hours
Because electricity is a direct cost
Because allocation bases are chosen randomly
questions.explanation: The best allocation base reflects what actually causes the cost to be incurred.

questions.question: A shipping company wants to determine the operating cost of each vessel. What is the most appropriate cost object?
questions.explanation: Each vessel is a separate cost object because management wants to measure its individual operating cost.

questions.question: Why is selecting an appropriate allocation base important?
options: It improves the accuracy of allocated costs
questions.explanation: A suitable allocation base distributes indirect costs more fairly among cost objects.

questions.question: A company allocates factory rent equally across all products, even though one product uses most of the factory space. What is the main problem with this approach?
options: Some products may receive an unfair share of the overhead costs
Factory rent becomes a direct cost
The products will all become equally profitable
questions.explanation: An inappropriate allocation base can distort product costs and lead to poor decisions.

questions.question: Which allocation base would generally be most appropriate for allocating maintenance costs on manufacturing equipment?
questions.explanation: Maintenance costs are often driven by machine usage, making machine hours an appropriate allocation base.

questions.question: A business wants to know whether one of its products is actually profitable. Why must indirect costs be allocated?
options: Because products consume shared resources that should be included in their total cost
Because revenue depends on indirect costs
questions.explanation: Ignoring indirect costs may make an unprofitable product appear profitable.

questions.question: A company chooses labor hours as the allocation base for electricity costs, even though electricity is mainly consumed by automated machines. What is the likely consequence?
options: Product costs may be distorted because the allocation base does not reflect actual resource consumption
No effect because any allocation base gives identical results
questions.explanation: The allocation base should closely reflect what actually causes the cost.

questions.question: Which statement best describes good cost allocation?
options: Indirect costs are allocated using a logical base that reflects how resources are consumed
Every indirect cost should be divided equally among all products
Indirect costs should never be allocated
Allocation is only necessary for tax purposes
questions.explanation: Good cost allocation uses allocation bases that closely match actual resource usage.

questions.question: A manufacturing company ignores indirect costs when pricing one of its products. What is the greatest risk?
options: The product may be sold below its true cost
The product becomes a direct cost
questions.explanation: Ignoring overhead costs can underestimate the true cost of a product, leading to prices that are too low.

questions.question: A company allocates administrative costs based on the number of employees in each department. Why might this be an appropriate allocation base?
options: Departments with more employees generally require more administrative support
Every allocation base produces identical results
questions.explanation: An allocation base should reflect how the shared resource is actually consumed.

questions.question: A ship management company allocates headquarters' administrative expenses equally across all vessels, even though some vessels require much more management support. What is the most likely consequence?
options: Some vessels may appear more or less profitable than they actually are
The administrative costs become direct costs
Every vessel will have identical operating costs
questions.explanation: Using an inappropriate allocation method can distort profitability analyses.

questions.question: Which statement best explains why indirect costs cannot usually be traced directly to a single product?
options: Because they support multiple products, departments or activities simultaneously
Because they are always fixed costs
Because they only occur once per year
Because they are paid by customers
questions.explanation: Indirect costs benefit multiple cost objects and therefore require allocation.

questions.question: A company chooses a poor allocation base that significantly overallocates overhead to one product. What business decision could be negatively affected?
questions.explanation: Incorrect product costs may lead managers to set inappropriate selling prices.

questions.question: Why should managers understand that allocated costs are estimates rather than exact measurements?
options: Because many indirect costs cannot be measured precisely for individual cost objects
Because accounting standards prohibit accurate calculations
Because all allocation methods produce identical results
questions.explanation: Indirect costs must often be distributed using reasonable assumptions rather than exact measurements.

questions.question: Which manager demonstrates the best understanding of cost allocation?
options: A manager who selects allocation bases that reflect how resources are actually consumed
A manager who divides every indirect cost equally among all products
A manager who ignores indirect costs completely
A manager who classifies every cost as direct
questions.explanation: Effective managers choose allocation methods that best represent actual resource consumption.

questions.question: Why is accurate cost allocation important when evaluating product profitability?
options: Because products should bear an appropriate share of the indirect costs required to produce them
Because only direct costs influence profitability
Because overhead costs never affect pricing decisions
Because profitability depends only on revenue
questions.explanation: Ignoring overhead costs may cause managers to believe an unprofitable product is profitable.

questions.question: A company introduces a more accurate allocation method and discovers that one of its best-selling products is actually generating very little profit. What should management do first?
options: Analyze why the product consumes so many shared resources before making decisions
questions.explanation: Managers should investigate the causes before deciding whether to change pricing, processes or the product itself.

questions.question: What is the ultimate objective of cost allocation?
options: To provide managers with more accurate cost information for pricing, budgeting, profitability analysis and decision-making
To maximize company revenue regardless of costs
To simplify financial statements by removing overhead
questions.explanation: Cost allocation exists to improve managerial decision-making by assigning shared costs as fairly and accurately as possible.
